natural_occurrence.description: Potassium chloride occurs naturally in the mineral sylvite and in underground salt deposits. It is also an essential electrolyte in all living organisms. Commercial production comes from mining potash deposits or extraction from sea water.

manufacturing.description: Extracted from underground potash deposits (mined as sylvinite ore and separated from sodium chloride) or produced by the reaction of potassium hydroxide with hydrochloric acid. Can also be recovered from sea water through evaporation and crystallization processes.

other_uses.industrial: Fertilizers (major use), oil well drilling fluids, chemical manufacturing.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Electrolyte replenisher, treatment for hypokalemia (low potassium), lethal injection component.
other_uses.household: Water softener salt (alternative to sodium chloride).

eu.conditions: Permitted as gelling agent and salt substitute in various food categories.

usa.notes: Widely used as reduced-sodium salt substitute and labeled as such.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (safe at levels used as salt substitute)

commonly_found_in: Low-sodium salt and salt substitutes (like 'Lite Salt', 'No Salt')
fun_facts: The main ingredient in 'low-sodium salt' products - replaces about 25-50% of the sodium chloride
Has a slightly bitter or metallic aftertaste compared to regular table salt, which some people find unpleasant
Can be DANGEROUS for people with kidney disease or taking certain medications (ACE inhibitors, potassium-sparing diuretics)
Your body needs potassium as much as sodium - it's an essential mineral for heart and muscle function
Mined from the same underground deposits as table salt, just from different mineral layers
The same chemical compound used in medical IV drips to treat low potassium (hypokalemia)
Controversial use: one of the drugs used in lethal injection executions (stops the heart)
